title: What the GBBC taught us about Bengaluru's bird diversity
excerpt: Our GBBC weekend showed us how much diversity can exist inside and around a university campus. From early-morning calls to unexpected sightings near built spaces, the count changed how we read everyday landscapes.
content:
## Why GBBC matters

The Great Backyard Bird Count gives students a practical way to contribute to citizen science. At TGA, we split into small teams and focused on repeatable observation routes.

## What we observed

We found that edge habitats near tree clusters and water accumulation points had the highest activity. Several members also noticed how quickly species richness shifted with time of day.

## Takeaways

Consistent observation, responsible field behavior, and better checklist discipline can produce strong local data. GBBC is not just an event for us - it is also a training ground for long-term biodiversity tracking.
author: Nivedita Rao

title: How to start birdwatching as a complete beginner
excerpt: Birdwatching can feel overwhelming at first, but it does not need expensive equipment or expert knowledge. A notebook, patience, and a simple routine are enough to begin.
content:
## Start with habit, not gear

Begin with short 20-minute sessions around the same route. Repetition helps your eyes and ears identify patterns.

## Learn common species first

Instead of chasing rare sightings, focus on common campus birds and their behavior. This builds confidence quickly.

## Keep your notes simple

Record date, time, location, weather, and one behavior note per species. Over time, this forms a meaningful personal dataset.
author: Arjun Menon

title: Our Kengeri lake cleanup: what we found and what it means
excerpt: The cleanup revealed recurring waste clusters and patterns that are easy to miss in one-time drives. Documenting what we picked up helped us think beyond collection and toward prevention.
content:
## What showed up most

Single-use plastics and mixed dry waste dominated shoreline edges. We also observed disposal patterns around access points with heavy foot traffic.

## Why data matters

By tagging waste categories and areas, we can report clearer findings to civic partners. This helps shape targeted interventions instead of generic campaigns.

## What comes next

TGA plans repeat cleanups paired with awareness sessions for students and nearby communities.
author: Samiksha Pai

title: Meet the 68 bird species living on our campus
excerpt: Our campus checklist crossed 68 bird species, including residents and seasonal visitors. The list highlights how even fragmented green spaces can support rich urban biodiversity.
content:
## Building the checklist

The list was compiled through repeated campus walks, validated observations, and community review among TGA members.

## Surprising patterns

Species presence shifted strongly between early mornings and late afternoons. Areas with layered vegetation consistently performed better than open lawns.

## Why this matters for RVU

The checklist supports evidence-based campus greening decisions and creates opportunities for student-led ecological monitoring.
author: Dharini N
